text: Functional programming is awesome, but functional programmers are bad marketers — jargon like currying, monads, and binding functors confuses people.
These concepts lie on a spectrum between the math of category theory / lambda calculus and their specific mapping to each language's implementation.
A function is a pure, elegant thing that takes an input and gives an output, and the same input always gives the same output.
What guarantees the same output forever is having no side effects and no state.
A pure function contains nothing inside it that isn't directly derived from the input.
Once you introduce state, the outcome of a function can vary based on that state.
Most programming paradigms are really just different takes on how you manage state — it's all about state.
Functional programming takes one extreme of the state question: there shall be no state.
You transform inputs into outputs by composing and connecting functions — hooking inputs and outputs into one long chain that achieves the program's goal.
In pure FP there are no objects or structures in memory that you mutate to track what's going on.
You're not allowed to mutate variables: once you set a value you cannot change it again.
Conventional iteration loops (for and while) are impossible in pure FP because they require mutating a variable that tracks how many times you've iterated.
The functional answer to 'how do you loop' is usually 'more functions' — when all you have is a hammer.
To loop functionally, pull the part you want to repeat out into its own function.
Replicate the for loop's index increment by creating a new variable (next index) instead of mutating index, since you can't mutate.
Repeat by having the function call itself recursively with the next index.
Without a stopping condition the recursion calls forever until it goes beyond the array size and throws an error.
The for loop's condition maps to: only execute the body if the condition (index still in range) is met, which breaks the recursion so all the functions return.
Recursion is just a fancier form of iteration.
verbatim: recursion is just a fancier form of iteration

text: The for loop's starting point maps to the first call into the recursive loop.
To return matching results, build an array with the current item at the front and the results of the deeper recursive calls appended to the end, preserving order.
Only append the current item if it matches the condition; otherwise pass through the previous result unchanged; base case returns empty when the list is fully processed.
This find-in-array pattern recurs everywhere; the only difference between such functions is the condition in the if statement.
Replace the three near-identical functions with one by passing in a function that returns whether an item should be included.
The passed-in condition function is called a predicate; the generalized function becomes a generic filter.
Problem: the userID-equals function needs two parameters (receipt and userID) but filter only passes the receipt, so the userID to compare against is unknown.
Solution: have the function take only the userID and construct and return a new function that takes a receipt and checks it; pass that returned function into filter.
The userID acts as a secret extra parameter bound to the returned function, which remembers that value and uses it later.
Currying: instead of handling the entire operation at once, return a function that handles the next part of the operation with the previous state bound to it.
It's ironic that FP is sold as so different from OOP, yet binding data this way creates an instance of a function with bound data — the mirror image of an OOP instance of data with bound functions.
Manual currying is an older approach; you can automate it in JavaScript and many languages using bind.
The modern preferred way is the lambda function: move the check right into an inline function.
With lambdas you can filter by user, filter by merchant, or do a more complex check such as the total being in a range.
map is a function that takes a function giving the desired result for each element and inserts that transformed element into the new array.
take(count) gives you up to 'count' elements from the array, and is chainable.
Chaining filter, map, and take creates a data pipeline: the code declares WHAT should happen while the HOW stays hidden in the fundamental functions.
These functional fundamentals (filter, map, take) are built directly into JavaScript.
Worked example goal: grab each receipt, look up its transaction, filter unsettled or mismatched ones, sort by the most egregious discrepancy, take the top 10, and display them in a dashboard.
Use the built-in array map with a lambda to transform each receipt into a result object holding the receipt, final transaction, and discrepancy.
Convert skip-over logic into a filter by inverting the conditions so they describe what to keep instead of what to ignore.
sort takes a comparator returning negative if a<b and positive if a>b; subtracting b from a sorts ascending, so subtract a from b to order biggest-first.
numbers: negative if a<b; positive if a>b

text: Use slice to take only the count number of elements at the end of the pipeline.
The whole procedural routine, rewritten as a functional pipeline, fits in only 12 lines.
Most programming jobs are just taking data from source A, doing transformation/filtering/sorting, and putting it in place B — a lot of the time we're just sophisticated plumbers.
Pure functional programming — where you cannot mutate any state or have any side effects — is kind of awful, and in a sense fake.
Computers fundamentally have state: the Von Neumann architecture is one giant state in memory with little instructions that mutate it.
Input is non-deterministic and any output forces some side effect, so pure statelessness can't fully exist.
Pure functional programming is still mostly academic.
What has happened is that the best parts of functional programming have been extracted and injected into our most popular languages.
Balance is key: adopting principles like reducing state and forming data pipelines where it makes sense brings huge benefit without the crazy restrictiveness of pure FP.
Expect these functional patterns to become more common.
Massively parallel systems don't rely on one big state of memory; the more cores you add, the more tracking shared state becomes a bottleneck.
Pure functional programming may have a new renaissance as parallelism grows.
Recurring affectionate address to the audience: 'dear functional bros, I see you, I hear you, and I love you.'
verbatim: dear functional Bros I see you I hear you and I love you

text: A satirical fake-startup ad ('Black Sky Financial') frames the receipts demo — a parody company that sues mom-and-pop restaurants over tip discrepancies.
After deriving the functional fundamentals by hand, you can remove unneeded variables to make the code more concise.
'Dependency injection' is a term the creator doesn't love because it sounds a lot fancier than it actually is.
Dependency injection is simply when code that uses another piece of code, instead of using it directly, has that code passed in; passing it in is the 'injection'.
verbatim: we inject the dependent code into the code that uses it

text: Though the idea is simple, it unlocks some very powerful side effects.
The running example is a business chat app where users message co-workers and send pictures and files; attachments go to an attachment service that stores, retrieves, and processes them.
Message text goes to a standard chat service built for speed (near real-time), while the attachment goes to the separate attachment service.
On upload, the attachment is stored on disk temporarily, processed in several ways, then uploaded to its final destination.
The default storage location is S3, Amazon's simple storage service for putting up and pulling down files.
A few client firms don't want their data permanently stored, so the service must handle multiple storage locations chosen by which company a user belongs to.
Most picky clients provide an SFTP server, but one client specifically wanted WebDAV.

The naive approach — extend the upload code with if-statements and have the caller pass in where to upload — intermingles SFTP, AWS, and WebDAV code in one class with many paths, making it ugly and hard to understand.

Because each destination needs different info (AWS keys vs SFTP address/private key vs WebDAV URL and auth), the naive upload forces a bunch of optional variables and comments, making it easy for the caller to make a mistake.

The naive approach forces the calling code to carry destination-specific context even though at that phase it just wants to upload; the code that actually knows the company is up at the start of the request.
The DI solution: create a storage interface with an upload method that does what the request handler wants.
Create three implementations of the storage interface, passing each one's configuration into its constructor — no optional variables, required values, and an error if you forget one.
Once the user is authenticated and their company is known, create the right storage and inject only that storage into the request handler.
The request handler isn't aware of which storage is passed in or where the file is going — it just knows it can call upload.
Since the construction code is too complex to sit in the handler, move it out to a factory whose input is the company configuration and whose output is a storage conforming to the interface.
Beyond final storage, an upload passes through stages: virus scan, image scaling, preview generation, and encryption.
The virus scanner checks files for signatures of obvious viruses.
If the file is an image it's scaled down to a max width of 2500 pixels, which is what's shown when a user clicks an image because it uses less bandwidth and loads faster.
Preview generation makes the thumbnail shown under an attachment so the user can see what it is without fully downloading it.
Files stored on S3 are pre-encrypted before upload, so a breach at Amazon only exposes encrypted files.
They currently use the ThreatProtect scanner but plan to switch to Synergy Security (better detection KPIs); the deal isn't finalized, so Synergy may only be tested in development, not production.
Create a shared interface for the two scanners and, on initialization, pick one and inject it: Synergy in development mode, the old ThreatProtect in production.
Image scaling (the sharp library) is wrapped in an image-scaler interface that also reports whether an attachment supports scaling, so the upload only scales when supported.
Preview generation, the most complex stage, uses a preview-generator interface taking the input file and returning a preview image, with implementations for documents, videos, and images.
The image preview generator reuses the same image scaler by injecting it in, rather than reimplementing scaling.
Since only one preview generator is needed per file type, inject a factory that takes the upload's mime type and returns the right generator, so the upload just asks the factory and uses it.
Encryption has one implementation (AES) but a per-user key from a key service, so inject the key service into the AES encryption and the AES encryption into the storage factory.
For an AWS-configured company the storage factory injects AES encryption into the new AWS storage, and the upload calls upload without knowing about the whole chain of connected functionality.
The complete architecture is configurable from one spot, making it super easy to change — switching to Synergy is two lines, and adding preview support for a new file type just slots in.
With no access to the key server on a local dev box, you can just inject a fixed key when running locally.
Injection basically lets you pick and choose from your compatible puzzle pieces and slot them in when you need them.
verbatim: pick and choose from our compatible puzzle pieces

text: Injection timing varies: some dependencies are resolved and injected at startup (most common), some when a request comes in, but the process is the same — code lists the dependencies it needs and you fulfill them.
The biggest reason to use interfaces and injection even with a single implementation is testing.

Because components talk through injected interfaces, you control what's used at each connection point — including injecting fake or mock implementations to slice and isolate sections of the architecture during testing.
To test the AWS storage class, inject a fake local S3 that pretends to be the cloud, call upload, and verify a file got uploaded.
Since encryption would block checking the file content, inject a mock encryption that disables encryption, so the test can fully verify uploading in isolation.
To test encryption itself, mock out the key store to return a controlled key instead of going to the real key service.
To integration-test AWS and encryption together, inject a fake keystore into the real encryption and the real encryption into the AWS storage.
A natural side effect of having nice code is that it's easy to test without needing to hack around the code structure.
If you find yourself asking 'how can I test a private method' or 'I need to set an internal variable to test', that's a signal you need to pull some part out and isolate it by injecting it instead.
verbatim: that's a signal that you maybe need to pull some stuff out

text: The creator truly believes that premature optimization is the root of all evil.
verbatim: premature optimization is the root of all evil

text: Most conversations about performance are a total waste of time — not because performance isn't important, but because people feel very, very strongly about it.
Performance is one element of a trade-off triangle whose other two corners are velocity and adaptability.
Velocity is how quickly one adds new features.
Adaptability is how well the system can change to new requirements.
Pure velocity — hacking something together by the shortest path, future maintainers be damned — gives immediate speed but creates technical debt that eventually weighs you to a halt.
Adaptability means writing code to enable change: reusable extensible components, beautifully crafted interfaces, and configurability.
With the right designs, adaptability can increase velocity by reducing the size of the changes needed to add new features.
Too much adaptability also hurts velocity: building for a bunch of use cases that never happen just ends up being wasted time.
Adding adaptability naturally adds abstraction and indirection, which usually hurts performance — but the trade-off is usually worth it for the adaptability gained.
Where to sit on the triangle depends on the stage of your project, not a fixed 'always aim for the middle' rule.
A feature-complete game near its ship date would focus on performance, accepting reduced velocity and adaptability; a game early in development would favor features and an extensible system.
Zuckerberg wrote Facebook in PHP — an awkward language that caused scaling issues; they later built a PHP-to-C++ compiler and then a PHP dialect called Hack.

Writing Facebook in inconsistent, inefficient PHP was the right move because it let Zuckerberg build quickly; once performance became a real problem, engineers solved it — starting in optimized C might have meant it never took off.
Performance is a problem, but it's not usually the first problem — be deliberate about which way you're leaning.
Macro performance is design-level, system-wide performance; micro performance is fine-tuned performance of a single module or function.
Premature optimization usually occurs for micro performance — typically the code-review comment that you should do X instead of Y because X is faster.
His own animation Python code linear-searches a big character array every frame and still renders the videos fast enough that it's not worth making faster.
You write code to solve a real-world problem, and getting to that solution faster is typically better than solving it slower with faster code.
C++ has ++i and i++; proponents claim ++i is faster because i++ must copy the value, since when part of a larger expression the increment must happen after evaluation.
You shouldn't blindly trust a claim that something is faster; verify it — which here meant disassembling the code.
For an int, pre-increment and post-increment disassemble to identical code (load into a register, add one, store back).
For a vector iterator, post-increment has four extra assembly lines to copy the iterator and call into the other operator.
But once optimization is turned on, those extra function calls completely disappear and you get identical code for both ++i and i++.
Measured on his MacBook, the slowest increment is 3.4 nanoseconds.
Ask whether the conversation is even worth it: if figuring out which is technically faster brings you joy, go for it, but it shouldn't end up in a code review because it doesn't matter.
He likes i++ because he thinks it looks prettier and sticks with it until someone measures that the app is too slow and that changing it is the fix.

Some argue functions are expensive, but rarely is the cost of a function so significant that removing it is the solution to a performance problem — and even then it doesn't prove the readability loss is worth it.
A big issue with making performance rules to follow is that the rules often have a lot of exceptions.
Example: a logged-in-users class searches an array with a for loop; a reviewer says to use a set for faster unique lookups.

Measuring shows the set is actually slower when only a few users are logged in (the normal case), likely because the array's integers sit adjacent in memory while the set allocates objects scattered around, reducing cache hits.
Until you've shown a function is the leading cause of performance issues, go with what's more readable; here the set is cleaner and less bug-prone, so he'd still choose it.
There's only one way to properly optimize: measure, try something, measure again.
Measuring is critical because your assumptions about what will make things faster can actually make them slower.
Data-structure selection is by far the most important factor, because the right data structure gives dramatically better results than the wrong one.
Think in '80% moves' first — changes that could give an 80% reduction — and often only data-structure changes get you that far.
If 80% moves aren't enough, use a profiler to find the hot spots — the functions that are most expensive.
A player named 'tossed' profiled GTA 5 Online without source code, found nearly all the time was spent parsing a JSON file, and a patch fixing just that improved load time by 70%.
Sometimes when things are slow it's just one silly thing slowing everything down.

A lot of performance comes from how code uses memory: allocating memory (a new object or array) slows critical sections because the system must find free chunks, and keeping critical elements close in memory exploits the CPU cache.
Don't worry about memory-level concerns until you know you have a performance problem and have tried other things first.

The ordered optimization recipe: have a real performance problem, measure it, make 80% moves by swapping data structures or moving to a faster algorithm, profile for hot spots, and worst case start reasoning about what the code does under the hood.
Spending three hours investigating a micro-optimization is time you could have spent building the actual product.
